mnemonics.title: Setup and Hold Time Remember
mnemonics.content: SHE (Setup = Hold's Elder) - Setup must happen before Hold, so Setup comes first chronologically

mnemonics.title: Clock Types Memory
mnemonics.content: CLOCKS: Crystal, LC/RC, Oscillator, Crystal Oscillator, Wave generator, Key components, Synchronous

mnemonics.title: Skew vs Jitter
mnemonics.content: SKEW = Spatial (different locations), JITTER = Temporal (time variation)

acronyms.usage: Used for clock multiplication and synchronization in processors
Used for clock deskewing and alignment
Component in PLL that generates variable frequency based on control voltage
Used to measure metastability failure rate in flip-flops
Low power technology commonly used for clock circuits

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two runners starting a race from different distances to the same finish line - the clock arrives at different times at different locations
Think of a ball balanced perfectly on top of a hill - it can stay temporarily but will eventually fall to either side (0 or 1)
Visualize a see-saw: 50% duty cycle means balanced, 70% means see-saw stays up longer
Imagine the second hand of a watch shaking/vibrating slightly - the time jumps forward and backward around the correct position

visualTips.visualize: Two traffic lights at an intersection that never turn green at the same time - preventing cars (data) from crashing

analogies.analogy: Like the heartbeat of the human body - it sets the rhythm for all biological processes and ensures organs work in synchronization
Like the time difference between two clocks in different rooms - one shows 3:00 while another shows 3:02 because they weren't synchronized properly
Like needing to be at the bus stop 5 minutes before the bus arrives (setup time) to catch it
Like needing to stay at the bus stop for 2 minutes after the bus arrives (hold time) to actually get on
Like a spinning coin - it doesn't immediately land on heads or tails but wobbles in between temporarily
